coredata.dc:title: Modified chitosan as a multi-functional material in eco-friendly formulation for anti-counterfeit applications
coredata.dc:creator: Muthamma, Kashmitha
Sunil, Dhanya
Shetty, Prakasha
Kulkarni, Suresh D.
Wagle, Shivananda
Alex, Angel Treasa
coredata.dc:description: Renewable resources and waste materials can be used as viable raw materials to prepare security formulations devoid of volatile organic compounds and would essentially offer environmental protection. Therefore, chitosan (deacetylated chitin), which is a marine waste material is modified through simultaneous N-conjugation with pyrene-1-carboxaldehyde (PCA) as an aggregation caused quenching fluorophore and fluorene-2-carboxaldehyde (FCA) to obtain a smart fluorescent chitosan-based Schiff base (FCSSB). The covalent attachment could immobilize pyrene onto the chitosan, and the subsequent diluting effect of the biopolymer framework could hinder the aggregation of pyrene and, thereby, the self-quenching of fluorescence. The modified FCSSB could serve as a multi-functional material; both as a binder and a fluorophore in an eco-friendly formulation using aqueous acetic acid as the mild solvent and glycerol as the viscosity regulator. The formulation is used as a security ink to obtain single/multilayer coating/s and further prints using flexography technique. The prints that appeared invisible in daylight exhibited a blue fluorescence under 370 nm UV illumination. Moreover, the ink film presented good rub resistance and colour stability after different cycles of UV exposure. Therefore, FCSSB formulation can be readily utilized as a security ink for information decryption and could also be processed into products of diverse forms, including ink pads, coatings, printed patterns, and films. This simple, but effective approach provides additional impetus to real-life applications of biopolymers from natural/waste materials in anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Fluorescence
Eco-friendly formulation
Flexography printing
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of chitosan Schiff base (FCSSB)
Determination of degree of substitution of CS
Ink formulation using FCSSB-20 as binder and fluorescent dye

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis scheme of FCSSB. The table lists the feed ratio and the weights of the raw materials used as well as the Schiff bases formed.

a) FTIR and b) UV–visible spectra of CS and FCSSBs. c) Solid-state emission spectra (λex = 420 nm) and d) CIE plots of CS and FCSSBs. e) Emission spectra (λex = 370 nm) and f) CIE plot of CS and FCSSB in 2% aqueous acetic acid.
a) TGA plots of CS and FCSSB-20. FESEM images of b) CS c) FCSSB-20.
a) Emission spectra (λex = 370 nm) and b) CIE plots of ink-coated paper samples. c) Emission spectra (λex = 370 nm) and d) CIE plots of coated and printed paper samples.

a) XRD spectra of CS, FCSSB-20 and the ink film. SEM images of b) unprinted and c) printed paper samples. AFM images of d) unprinted and e) printed paper samples. f) Emission spectra (λex = 370 nm) of the print upon varying number of UV light exposure cycles.

Colorimetric (L*, a*, b*) colour space of a) prints with different exposure cycles under UV 300 W/30 m/min and b) rub tested prints. Photographs in daylight and under 365 nm UV light of c) handwritten character ‘CS’, fingerprint, and d) thin film.
Comparison of colorimetric values of printed samples upon UV light exposure and after rub test.

head.ce:title: Modified chitosan as a multi-functional material in eco-friendly formulation for anti-counterfeit applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Applied Nanosciences, Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Applied Nanosciences, Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal, 576104, Karnataka, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Manipal Technologies Limited, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Manipal Technologies Limited, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Manipal College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Manipal College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Renewable resources and waste materials can be used as viable raw materials to prepare security formulations devoid of volatile organic compounds and would essentially offer environmental protection. Therefore, chitosan (deacetylated chitin), which is a marine waste material is modified through simultaneous N-conjugation with pyrene-1-carboxaldehyde (PCA) as an aggregation caused quenching fluorophore and fluorene-2-carboxaldehyde (FCA) to obtain a smart fluorescent chitosan-based Schiff base (FCSSB). The covalent attachment could immobilize pyrene onto the chitosan, and the subsequent diluting effect of the biopolymer framework could hinder the aggregation of pyrene and, thereby, the self-quenching of fluorescence. The modified FCSSB could serve as a multi-functional material; both as a binder and a fluorophore in an eco-friendly formulation using aqueous acetic acid as the mild solvent and glycerol as the viscosity regulator. The formulation is used as a security ink to obtain single/multilayer coating/s and further prints using flexography technique. The prints that appeared invisible in daylight exhibited a blue fluorescence under 370 nm UV illumination. Moreover, the ink film presented good rub resistance and colour stability after different cycles of UV exposure. Therefore, FCSSB formulation can be readily utilized as a security ink for information decryption and could also be processed into products of diverse forms, including ink pads, coatings, printed patterns, and films. This simple, but effective approach provides additional impetus to real-life applications of biopolymers from natural/waste materials in anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Modern seafood processing generates large amounts of crustacean shells as waste product, and their inadequate disposal negatively impacts the environment and human health. This marine food waste contains chitin, the second-most abundant natural polysaccharide that could be converted to several high-value products through suitable modifications [1]. Chitosan (CS), which is easily obtained from chitin comprises of N-acetyl-glucosamine and deacetylated glucosamine as the repeating units in its structural framework. This biopolymer has attracted researchers due to its unique intrinsic physicochemical properties compared to synthetic polymers [2]. However, though CS is hydrophilic, solubility issue limits its practical utility. Interestingly, the hydroxyl and amino functional groups that decorates the macromolecule backbone can be chemically modified to achieve desired properties for various applications [3]. Some typical CS modification reactions are O-carboxymethylation, N-phthaloylation, alkylation, N-carboxy alkylation, acylation, and Schiff base formation, in addition to graft copolymerization, sulfonation, phosphorylation, and quaternization [2,3]. The linear polysaccharide exhibits excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability even after modification. Several reported studies support the application of CS in diverse industries, including food, agriculture, textile, medical, and packaging [4–7]. Though CS-derived functional materials have been explored in the recent past, there are no detailed reports on the prospective use of CS in anti-counterfeit technology. Few literature evidences suggest the successful usage of CS as a binder in ink formulations: CS-based bio-inks developed for extrusion-based 3D bioprinting [8–10], whereas edible screen/flexographic-printing ink with suitable viscosity as well as excellent drying feature, adhesion to substrate and tinting strength prepared for prospective safe application in food package printing industry [11–13]. However, the scope of CS as a functional biomaterial in anti-counterfeiting applications is yet to be investigated.

In this fast-evolving technological era, the security of stored information, protection against forgery, and identification of genuine goods/documents are of immense significance. The counterfeit products or imitated goods, currencies, products, and documents are difficult to identify and differentiate from the original. The duplicate banknotes/documents can create an economic loss for the consumers/governments, whereas fake cosmetics/medicinal/pharmaceutical products can severely affect human health. Therefore, various anti-counterfeit measures have been developed, and the use of fluorescent security inks, films, and fibres are widely used for data storage and identification of genuine documents [14–18]. Various classes of molecules, such as lanthanides, carbon/quantum dots, plasmonic materials, metal-organic frameworks, organic molecules, and polymers, have been used as fluorescent dyes/pigments along with resins, binders, solvents, and other additives in security ink formulations [19]. In the present study, we explore the N-modification of biopolymer CS using pyrene-1-carbaldehyde (PCA) and fluorene-2-carbaldehyde (FCA) to obtain a smart fluorescent material for anti-counterfeit application.

Pyrene is a symmetric polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon with yellow solid-state fluorescence and strong blue emission in solution with a long emission lifetime and high quantum yield. The flat electron-rich molecule can act as a strong electron donor. It can be combined with several materials to construct electron donor-acceptor systems with superior emission characteristics. However, though pyrene intensely emits in its dilute solutions, the aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effect of the molecule is well-established due to excimer formation through π-π stacking [20]. Hence, it is not recommended as a fluorophore for solid-state anti-counterfeit applications. Conversely, the occurrence of this ACQ luminophore in lesser concentration in polymeric host systems can realize good solid-state fluorescence [20]. Moreover, low amounts of pyrene will not significantly alter the intrinsic features such as biocompatibility, biodegradability, mechanical features, solubility, and processibility of the host polymer material [21]. Hence, pyrene was chosen as one of the fluorescent entities for the N-modification of CS. Fluorene is yet another rigid polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon in which the methylene bridge forces the two benzene rings to be planar with an extended π-electron delocalization to display efficient violet-blue solid-state fluorescence emission [22]. With this background, we propose the modification of CS using pyrene-1-carbaldehyde (PCA) and fluorene-2-carbaldehyde (FCA) to obtain N-substituted CS (FCSSB) with good fluorescence properties. A very simple reaction protocol was followed for the synthesis of FCSSB. Its use as both binder and fluorophore in a cost-effective, biocompatible, environment-friendly ink formulation for anti-counterfeit application is further explored. The water-based biocompatible ink formulation is used to obtain coatings and flexography prints. Furthermore, the lightfastness and abrasion resistance of the prints are examined through colorimetric studies. The usage of the formulation in ink pads and preparation of flexible films are also explored.

The principal raw materials including CS, PCA, and FCA were procured from Sigma. The solvents/chemicals were obtained from Finar and Spectrochem Chemicals Pvt. Ltd. and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of chitosan Schiff base (FCSSB)
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic route for the synthesis of FCSSB is shown in Fig. 1 . About 0.2 g CS was added to 100 mL 2% aqueous acetic acid and stirred at room temperature for six hours to form a clear, viscous solution. FCA and PCA (feed ratio as per Fig. 1) dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol were added dropwise into the CS solution with continuous stirring and refluxed at 50 °C for six hours. The reaction mixture was neutralized using a 5% sodium hydroxide solution; the yellow-colored precipitate was filtered and washed several times with water and ethanol to remove the unreacted aldehydes. The product was dried overnight at 60 °C in a vacuum oven [23].

The vibrational spectra of chitosan Schiff bases (FCSSB-10 and FCSSB-20) were recorded using ATR Shimadzu-IR Spirit analyzer. The 1800 Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer and JASCO spectrofluorometer FP 8300 were used to study the absorption and emission features. Rigaku Miniflex 600 X–ray powder diffractometer with CuKα radiation source and Ni–filter was used to record the diffractograms of samples with a scattering range varying from 2θ = 5° to 80° at 4° per minute. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of FCSSB was conducted in the temperature range of 20–800 °C using TGA4000 PerkinElmer thermogravimetric analyzer at a heating rate of 20 °C per minute. Carl Zeiss Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) was used to capture the images of solid FCSSBs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of degree of substitution of CS
ce:section.ce:para:
The free amine content (NH2) of CS and FCSSBs was estimated using the potentiometric titration method to determine the degree of deacetylation (%DD) of CS and the degree of substitution (%DS) of the aldehydes on the CS backbone. A weighed amount of CS/FCSSBs was added into 0.1 N HCl and stirred at room temperature for 12 h to obtain adequate hydration and further titrated against 0.1 N NaOH [23,24]. The %DD was calculated using eqs. (1) and (2).
(1)
w
N
H 2
=
V × c × 100 × 0.016
W dry
(2)
N
H 2
=
203.195 × w
NH 2
16.0262 + 0.42037 × w
NH 2

Where v is the volume of NaOH consumed between two rapid variations in the pH, c is the concentration of NaOH, and wdry is the dry weight of the samples. NH2 and w(NH2) were estimated as percentage values. The %DS of FCSSB was calculated using the Eq. (3).
(3)
% DS = %
DD
CS
− %
DD
FCSSB

About 1 × 105 Vero cells were seeded in a 96-well microtitre plate and incubated at 37 °C in 5% CO2 atmosphere. After 24 h, supernatant was removed, the monolayer was washed with medium, and the cells were treated with FCSSB-20 at 10–100 ppm. After 48 h of incubation, the media was discarded, 50 μL of MTT was added to each well, and the plates were gently shaken and further incubated for 3 h. The supernatant was discarded, 50 μL of DMSO was added, the plates were gently shaken, and the absorbance was measured using an Elisa microplate reader (BioTek ELx800MS) at 540 nm. The percentage of viable cells was calculated using Eq. (4)
(4)
% Cell viability =
Test absorbance − Blank absorbance
Vehicle control absorbance − Blank absorbanc e
× 100

The X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) data of the dried ink film was collected using Rigaku Miniflex 600 with 1.54 Å CuKα radiation and 2θ ranging from 5° to 80° at 4° per minute as scan rate. UV dull paper was used as the coating/printing substrate, which was obtained from Manipal Press Pvt. Ltd., Manipal, India. K-bar coater - zero was used to coat the ink on the paper substrate. A 105 × 297 mm solid patch was printed using RK Flexiproof 100 with 9.5 BCM Anilox, chambered doctor blade inking system, and photopolymer plate at 75 m/min. The prints were dried at room temperature for subsequent studies. Xrite I1 Pro spectrophotometer was used to measure the L*, a*, b*, density, and ΔE values, whereas Hari Impex LAB UV 83002 instrument was used to verify the colour stability of the prints. The abrasion resistance of the ink film on the substrate was checked for 100 rubs using a Sutherland rub tester with 4 lbs. weight. The surface morphology of the printed and non-printed paper samples was examined using Carl Zeiss EVO 18 analytical SEM and INNOVA SPM Atomic Force microscope (AFM) instruments.

The %DS is an important parameter while assessing the conversion of CS to its modified derivatives. Therefore, the %DD of CS was determined experimentally through the potentiometric titration method, and the value obtained was 73.3. Further, the %DS of PCA and FCA onto the CS backbone was determined as 9.3 and 14.2 for FCSSB-10 and FCSSB-20, respectively, from the titration curves presented in Fig. S1. Fig. 2a depicts the FTIR spectral traces for CS and the FCSSB derivatives (FCSSB-10, FCSSB-20). The broad band observed at 3200–3600 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of amine and hydroxyl groups. The imine bond stretching and NH2 bending vibrational bands are observed at 1645 cm−1 and 1555 cm−1 [25]. The bands between 1200 and 1000 cm−1 are of the glycoside CC, CO and C-O-C stretching vibrations [23,24]. The UV–visible spectra were recorded by dissolving 10 mg of CS/FCSSBs in 5 mL of 2% acetic acid in a quartz cell with wavelength ranging from 200 to 800 nm. From Fig. 2b, the absorption around 230 and 320 nm in FCSSBs could be ascribed to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively [26]. The coupling of PCA and FCA to the CS backbone generated a new conjugation system compared to pure CS [23–25], which resulted in intense absorptions from the modified polymers compared to the unmodified CS. As expected, the absorption intensity was higher in FCSSB-20 due to the higher aldehyde substitution on the biopolymer backbone.

Further, the solid-state emission of 25 mg of CS/FCSSBs was recorded as presented in Fig. 2c upon excitation at 420 nm (Fig. S2a) and compared to that of PCA and FCA (Fig. S2b). The FCSSBs displayed a maximum emission at 542 nm corresponding to the pyrene moiety and another weak emission at 495 nm corresponding to the fluorene unit. The CIE plot in Fig. 2d indicates greenish-yellow emissions from both the solid-state FCSSBs. The intensity of both the emission peaks increased with an increase in %DS of the aldehydes onto the CS backbone, with a small shift in the emission colour as perceived from the CIE plot. Furthermore, the emission of 10 mg CS/FCSSBs in 5 mL of 2% aqueous acetic acid was recorded at 370 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 2e). Interestingly, the weaker peak at 495 nm observed in the solid-state spectra displayed a blue shift with the emission maximum changing to 470 nm. The CIE plot in Fig. 2f indicates the blue emission for FCSSBs in acetic acid solution. The intensity of the emission peak at 470 nm increased without any blue/red shift with an increase in %DS, whereas the intensity at 544 nm decreased with a blue shift to 542 nm. Generally, aggregation is favoured by the pyrene fluorophore, thereby quenching its solid-state fluorescence. However, the covalent immobilization of pyrene unit onto the CS skeleton in combination with the diluting effect of the biopolymer might have restricted the aggregation and, thereby the self-quenching of pyrene in solid FCSSB [27]. There might be a critical amount of pyrene concentration beyond which aggregation might occur [20], which needs further investigation. Based on the excellent solid-state emission features, FCSSB-20 was chosen for further studies to formulate a security ink for anti-counterfeit applications.

TGA analysis can provide insights into the temperature-dependent phase transitions and, in turn, the thermal stability of FCSSB-20. The TGA curves for the CS and FCSSB-20 (Fig. 3a) show two regular transitions. The first depression at 100 °C in CS and modified CS might be due to the loss of absorbed moisture, which is around 7% and 9% of the polymer weights, respectively. The weight loss could also be due to the loss of the amine groups to form crosslinked fragments [28]. The second depression from 220 to 350 °C could be due to the thermal degradation of either the pyranose rings of the biopolymer framework to form a complex adduct [23], or a new crosslinked material formed through thermal crosslinking reactions that occur in the initial stage of the degradation process [28]. Further, the FESEM images (Fig. 3b and c) were analysed to examine the surface morphology of FCSSB-20 compared to the unmodified biopolymer. A less porous surface texture is perceived on observing the CS microstructures. In contrast, the surface roughness and porous nature were found to increase in the FCSSB-20 after the modification process, which could be due to the interruption of the CS polymeric chain upon conjugation of its amino group with PCA and FCA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ink formulation using FCSSB-20 as binder and fluorescent dye
ce:section.ce:para: The FCSSB-20 was used as the main component to formulate an eco-friendly water-based ink for flexography printing. Initially, FCSSB-20 was added to 0.25% aqueous acetic acid and probe sonicated to achieve a homogenous solution. Further, glycerol was added and sonicated to obtain the final ink with the desired viscosity of 0.183 Pa.s as measured using a Zahn cup. The final formulation with FCSSB-20 (weight percentage: 2.88), 0.25% aqueous acetic acid (87.95%), and glycerol (9.17%) had a pH close to 7. Initially, a small amount of the ink was coated on UV-dull paper using a K-bar coater for preliminary visual testing. The coating appeared almost invisible in daylight and showed blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. The emission spectra recorded (at λex = 370 nm) for the coated paper sample, as displayed in Fig. 4a, exhibited an intensification in fluorescence with an increasing number of coatings (C1-C3). Further, the CIE plots were recorded (Fig. 4b) for these samples to evaluate the ink performance in terms of fluorescence emission colour with increasing coating thickness. The CIE chromaticity coordinates (x, y) values of C1, C2, and C3 were found to be (0.219, 0.250), (0.233, 0.285), and (0.239, 0.304), respectively. It was observed that the chromaticity diagram presented a colour shift from blue in C-1 toward greenish yellow in C-3. This result could be because the emission at 542 nm increased more than that at 470 nm, with increasing thickness of the ink film.

The prepared formulation was used as an ink to print on UV dull paper through the flexography technique, and the print properties were studied. The emission spectrum of the print, as displayed in Fig. 4c, showed a blue emission due to a thin ink film layer compared to a thicker layer formed during coating. The CIE chromaticity coordinates (x,y) values of C1 were (0.210, 0.250), and for P1 were (0.182, 0.169), respectively (Fig. 4d). The CIE plots also support the earlier observation that the emission displays a minor shift with an increase in ink film thickness on the paper substrate. The XRD plots of the ink film, as portrayed in Fig. 5a, were compared with that of CS and FCSSB-20. The XRD patterns suggested a semi-amorphous nature of CS with the characteristic peaks around 10° and 20° indexed to the (020) and (110) planes [25]. The diffractogram of FCSSB-20 also showed similar peaks with increased intensities, indicating a more crystalline nature after N-modification. However, a less intense broad peak around 22° was perceived for the ink film, possibly due to the destruction in the intra- and inter-molecular hydrogen bonding resulting in an amorphous structure [29,30].

Further, the SEM images for the unprinted and printed paper samples were obtained as shown in Fig. 5b and c. The prints displayed a highly smooth surface compared to the blank substrate surface [31]. This smooth texture could be because of the filling of the ink into the fibrous cellulose structure of the paper during printing. The surface properties of the printed sample were also verified using AFM, and the microscopic images are portrayed in Fig. 5d and e. The average surface roughness (Ra), root mean square roughness (Rq), and maximum roughness depth (Rmax) values demonstrate the reduction in surface roughness of the printed paper due to the uniform deposition of the ink film.

The lightfastness of the prints are very crucial for their real-life long-term applications, and if less can result in the fading of the colour, or change in the shade and even the disappearance of the colour eventually [32,33]. Therefore, the colour stability of the ink formulated using FCSSB-20 was investigated after exposing the prints of 1.5 cm × 1.5 cm dimension under UV curing instrument equipped with a 30 m belt and 300 W UV light for varying cycles: 10 (P-10), 20 (P-20), 30 (P-30), 40 (P-40) and 50 (P-50). The emission spectra for these samples were recorded (λex = 370 nm) and are depicted in Fig. 5f. Though the emission intensity of UV-exposed prints slightly decreased upon increasing UV exposure, it is observed that there was no drastic decline in emission even under 50 cycles of high UV exposure. These results suggest good lightfastness of the ink film on the substrate, which is also supported by the CIE plots (fig. S3).

The CIELAB colour spaces are intended to approximate human vision and are used to observe the chromaticity parameters of coated and printed substrates. The CIELAB recommends L*, a*, b* for colour description: L* indicates lightness with values from 0 to 100, 0 for black, and 100 for white. The two chromatic components a* and b* has values ranging from −120 to 120, where positive and negative a* values specify green and red, whereas positive and negative b* values point to yellow and blue, respectively. ΔE provides the colour differences and is calculated using eq. (5). The print is invisible if the ΔE value = 0–2; recognizable by experts if ΔE = 2–3, and any individual can recognize if ΔE > 3 [34,35].
(5)
ΔE =
Δ
L ∗
2
+
Δ
a ∗
2
+
Δ
b ∗
2
where (ΔL ∗) = L⁎
(u) – L⁎; (Δa ∗) = a ⁎
(u) – a⁎; (Δb) = b ⁎
(u) – b⁎ are the differences between the printed and (u) the unprinted paper samples [36].

The L*, a*, b*, ΔE, and optical densities (D) values recorded for the prints are presented in Table 1 . From the data, the L* value of 94.5 for the blank paper is close to 100, indicating white colour. The negative a* values indicate green, and as the positive b* value dominates a* values, the colour observed for the printed paper is more toward yellow. The increasing b* values with increasing UV exposure cycles suggest increased yellowness of the UV-exposed prints. Though the ΔE values increased for the prints that were subjected UV light exposure cycles, all the values being <3 suggested good light fastness of the ink. The L*, a*, b* values are represented in the two-dimensional CIE diagram in Fig. 6a and provide clear evidence for the good light fastness of the ink layer after long and powerful UV exposure of the prints.

The abrasion/rub resistance describes the extent to which an original print remains intact upon continuous scuffing and rubbing. The rub test measures the damage that can occur in print from repeated rubbing. The ability of the FCSSB-20 ink film to withstand the effects of rubbing and scuffing was determined by rubbing an 8 cm × 5 cm dimension strip of the printed sample against an unprinted sample. The Sutherland rub tester was set from 0 to 100 rubs, and the L*, a*, b * values were recorded after each set of rubs. The Table 1 lists the colorimetric values of the samples tested for abrasion resistance. The insignificant changes in the values suggest that the ink film is stable from 0 to 100 rubs. Fig. 6b also indicates the good strength of the ink film on the paper substrate.

The bright fluorescence and excellent solubility of FCSSB-20 in aqueous acetic acid as a mild solvent was well-utilized to realize an eco-friendly water-based formulation devoid of any volatile organic compounds. The formulation could be used as a security ink and efficiently applied for information encryption applications (Fig. 6c). It can also be used to combat counterfeiting as i) fluorescent tags and glossy strips, ii) to develop anti-counterfeit patterns including security bar codes, holograms and watermarks, iii) to detect fingerprints in forensic investigations, and iv) to authenticate currency notes, security cards and documents, as the prints/patterns will appear invisible in daylight, but glow under UV light.

Further, the potential utility of the formulation in ink pads for obtaining human fingerprints on important documents was explored. Therefore, initially the inherent biocompatibility of the FCSSB-20 was checked using MTT assay. It is observed from the concentration-dependent cytotoxicity study (fig. S4), that the Schiff base displays 70% cell viability up to 100 ppm concentration. Fig. 6c depicts the fingerprint obtained using the ink formulation on paper in daylight and a long-lasting, clear fluorescent fingerprint when viewed under 365 nm UV light. Moreover, the ink could be easily cleaned from fingers using water [37–39]. In contrast, the dried fingerprint on the paper could not be washed away by water, which might be due to the interaction between the cellulose fibres of the paper and the OH and NH groups present on FCSSB, preventing the dispersion of ink by the water molecules [39]. Thus, FCSSB can be a safe alternative for traditional inks in ink pads.

Furthermore, the FCSSB was easily fabricated into flexible fluorescent films using a solution casting technique. The FCSSB dissolved in 2% acetic acid was poured into a glass petri dish and was allowed to dry overnight in an oven at 50 °C. Upon cooling, the films were removed using forceps. Fig. 6d shows the photographs of the flexible films displaying yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light.

In summary, a smart multi-functional fluorescent material, FCSSB has been prepared through a one-step easy protocol by conjugating pyrene-1-carboxaldehyde and fluorene-2-carboxaldehyde into the chitosan framework. The biopolymer-based material was characterized by FTIR, XRD, UV–Visible spectral traces and fluorescence emission studies. The modified chitosan material displayed greenish-yellow fluorescence in solid state upon illumination with a 370 nm UV light source, whereas a blue fluorescence emission was observed from its aqueous acetic acid solution. The bright solid-state emission could be attributed to the synergistic influence of the restricted mobility of pyrene and the destruction of π-π stacked aggregates when the ACQ unit is covalently bonded to the chitosan skeleton. The Schiff base was further used as a multi-functional material: a binder and a fluorescent dye to formulate an ink for obtaining coatings and flexo-prints on UV-dull paper substrates. The prints appeared invisible in daylight and displayed good scratch/rub resistance, lightfastness, chromatic values, and blue fluorescence upon UV light illumination, demonstrating the potential of FCSSB to be used in the security printing industry. Moreover, the biopolymer-based material could be easily fabricated into various usable prototypes such as flexible films, and inks for security coatings/prints, food packaging, and anti-counterfeiting based on UV-fluorescence responses.

Kashmitha Muthamma: conducted the experiments, analysed and interpreted the data, and prepared the draft manuscript. Dhanya Sunil: proposed the research concept, supervised the experiments, and revised the manuscript. Suresh D Kulkarni: supervised and analysed the fluorescence measurements. Shivananda Wagle: printing-related studies. Prakasha Shetty: manuscript preparation. Angel Treasa Alex: provided MTT assay results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.reactfunctpolym.2023.105603.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Seafood waste: a source for preparation of commercially employable chitin/chitosan materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Yadav, P. Goswami, K. Paritosh, M. Kumar, N. Pareek, V. Vivekanand, Seafood waste: a source for preparation of commercially employable chitin/chitosan materials, Bioresour. Bioprocess. 6 (2019) 1–20. doi:10.1186/s40643-019-0243-y.
